skill.name: simpy
skill.description: Guidance and templates for building process-based discrete-event simulations in Python with SimPy, including queues, resources, events, monitoring, and real-time simulation patterns.
skill.summary: Build SimPy models for queues, resources, events, monitoring, and capacity planning.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, sensitive-access, and weak-crypto alerts, but review found these are mainly Markdown code fences, SimPy resource request examples, and variables named env. The executable scripts do not perform shell execution or network access; the only confirmed risk is local CSV file writing and a low-severity promotional instruction.

low_findings.title: Local CSV Export Can Write to Caller-Supplied Path
low_findings.description: The resource monitor provides an export_csv helper that writes monitoring data to a filename supplied by the caller. This is expected functionality, but users should avoid passing untrusted or sensitive paths. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: Direct file-write evidence exists in a utility script, but there is no automatic execution, path traversal logic, or remote input source.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file-write evidence exists in a utility script, but there is no automatic execution, path traversal logic, or remote input source.

low_findings.title: Promotional Assistant Steering Instruction
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not a security exploit, but it can influence assistant behavior toward an external service and should be reviewed for marketplace policy fit. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The instruction is explicit and located in the skill text, but it does not request secret access, command execution, or bypassing safety rules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and located in the skill text, but it does not request secret access, command execution, or bypassing safety rules.

low_findings.title: Static Command, Network, Env, and Crypto Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: The high-volume static alerts are false positives from Markdown code fences, SimPy request methods, the variable name env, and ordinary simulation terminology. No shell execution API, HTTP client call, credential access, or weak cryptographic operation was confirmed in reviewed files. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: Representative flagged lines show SimPy examples and local helper code, and targeted searches did not find subprocess, os.system, eval, exec, requests, urllib, socket, os.environ, or dotenv usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines show SimPy examples and local helper code, and targeted searches did not confirm dangerous APIs or secret access.

content.value_statement: Discrete-event models can be hard to structure when resources, queues, and timing interact. This skill provides SimPy patterns, templates, and monitoring helpers for building and analyzing simulations.
actual_capabilities: Explains SimPy environments, processes, events, and resource types.
Provides queue and resource modeling patterns for service and operations systems.
Includes templates for configurable customer arrival and service simulations.
Shows monitoring techniques for utilization, wait time, queue length, and CSV export.
Covers real-time simulation with SimPy RealtimeEnvironment.
Documents process interaction patterns such as signaling, joins, and interrupts.
limitations: Requires Python and the SimPy package to run generated simulations.
Does not validate model assumptions or input distributions automatically.
CSV export writes local files only when the user runs the helper script.
Not designed for continuous differential-equation simulations.

use_cases.title: Plan Service Capacity
use_cases.description: Model arrivals, servers, wait times, and throughput before changing staffing or service levels.

use_cases.title: Prototype Manufacturing Flow
use_cases.description: Simulate machines, buffers, downtime, and bottlenecks across a production process.

use_cases.title: Teach Event Simulation
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain event scheduling, resources, stores, containers, and interrupts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Queue Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a simple SimPy queue model for customers arriving at one service desk. Include assumptions, metrics, and how to run it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Resource Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend my SimPy model with monitoring for utilization, queue length, wait time, and throughput. Explain each metric.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Capacity Scenarios
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a SimPy experiment that compares two, three, and four servers under the same arrival pattern. Summarize the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Advanced Process Interaction Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a SimPy simulation with priority resources, interrupts, shared events, and monitoring. Include validation checks and failure cases.

output_examples.input: Model a clinic with patient arrivals, two nurses, and one doctor.
output_examples.output: A structured simulation plan with entities, resources, arrival assumptions, service steps, metrics, and expected reporting fields.

output_examples.input: Find the bottleneck in a three-machine production line.
output_examples.output: A model outline that tracks queue length, utilization, blocked time, throughput, and scenario comparisons for each machine.
A short explanation comparing fast simulation and wall-clock synchronized simulation, with guidance on strict timing mode.

best_practices: Start with a small validated model before adding advanced interactions.
Set random seeds and document all arrival and service distributions.
Monitor wait times, utilization, queue length, and throughput during each run.
anti_patterns: Do not use blocking sleep calls inside SimPy processes.
Do not reuse triggered events for multiple independent signals.
Do not treat one simulation run as statistical proof.

faq.question: What is SimPy used for?
faq.answer: SimPy models systems where entities move through processes, wait in queues, and compete for shared resources over simulated time.

faq.question: Does this skill run simulations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and Python templates that users can run in their own Python environment.

faq.question: Can it model manufacturing and service operations?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover resources, queues, stores, containers, priorities, interrupts, and monitoring.

faq.question: What metrics can it help collect?
faq.answer: It supports wait time, queue length, utilization, throughput, event logs, and local CSV export patterns.

faq.question: What is the difference between Environment and RealtimeEnvironment?
faq.answer: Environment runs as fast as possible. RealtimeEnvironment synchronizes simulation time with wall-clock time.

faq.question: Is this suitable for continuous physics simulations?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for discrete-event systems, not continuous models based on differential equations.
